data.title: Markets will see ‘one of the best periods in our life’ in the next 1.5-2 years – Findstrat’s Tom Lee
data.description:
Retail investors are poised to drive the next leg of the stock market rally as they begin moving capital back into equities following a period of war-related caution, according to Tom Lee, Fundstrat’s head of research.

In an interview with CNBC, Lee explained that while these investors initially pulled back during the buildup to the U.S.-Israel conflict with Iran, they are now “beginning to take money off the sidelines and buy stocks.”

He predicted retail investors will ultimately end up “chasing stock rally” for solid fundamental reasons, including rising earnings estimates.

The initial retail response to the Iran conflict was uncharacteristically risk-averse, breaking from the traditional “buy the dip” mentality that defined investor behavior during previous market disruptions like tariff concerns.

Lee attributed this hesitance to “policy puzzlement,” noting that investors “didn’t really know how big this war could become” and feared gasoline price (XB1:COM) spikes could trigger a recession.

“I think investors viewed the war, and the start of the war, as a time to take risk off the table,” Lee said, pointing to heavy selling in software stocks (IGV), (XSW) and the Magnificent Seven (MAGS) tech names.

While retail investors remained on the sidelines, hedge funds moved early to add risk back into their portfolios.

Lee confirmed this trend through Fundstrat’s client surveys and noted that the major “downside tail risks have been removed for the war.” This institutional activity has set the stage for retail participation to accelerate.

Despite ongoing public concern about gasoline prices (XB1:COM), Lee argued the U.S. consumer is in better shape than sentiment surveys indicate.

He emphasized that inflation-adjusted gasoline prices “aren’t nearly the burden they were five years ago, 10 years ago, even at the '08 peak.”

Furthermore, Lee noted that “the war is stimulating the economy,” pointing to improvements in earnings estimates, ISM data, and the jobs report as evidence of underlying strength.

Lee remains overweight on U.S. equities (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI), viewing the American market as the premier “growth index” for global investors seeking opportunity.

He highlighted that “the U.S.'s relative position has really been strengthened by what’s been exposed by supply chains through this war,” combined with continued American leadership in tech (XLK), healthcare (XLV), and fintech (FINX), (ARKF), (BPAY) innovation. Rather than seeing U.S. valuations contract, Lee believes the multiple should actually expand.

Looking beyond near-term challenges, including a new Fed chair transition, Lee offered a remarkably bullish long-term forecast.

He anticipates both earnings growth and multiple expansion could drive markets higher, predicting the coming 18-24-month period “might be one of the best we’ve ever seen in our life.”

data.title: BlackBerry jumps amid expanded Nvidia partnership aimed at robotics
data.description:
• BlackBerry (BB) shares jumped more than 12.5% on Monday after it announced an expanded partnership with Nvidia (NVDA) for robotics and edge computing.
  • The expanded relationship will see BlackBerry's QNX OS for Safety 8.0 integrated into Nvidia's IGTX Thor and Halos Safety Stack, BlackBerry said. This will bring QNX's real‑time operating system to Nvidia's safety platform to support regulated, AI‑enabled systems across robotics, medical technologies, industrial applications and more, the company said in a statement.
  • "As robotics, medical, and industrial systems become more autonomous and software defined, safety and determinism cannot be afterthoughts," said John Wall, President, QNX. "Integrating QNX OS for Safety 8.0 with NVIDIA IGX Thor and NVIDIA Halos Safety Stack brings together a trusted real‑time safety foundation and a powerful functional safety platform for edge AI. This expanded collaboration builds on our work with the NVIDIA DRIVE AGX Thor Development Kit and extends the same proven architecture from automotive into the next wave of regulated, intelligent systems."

data.title: China's clean energy exports surge in wake of Iran war - Ember
data.description:
China’s (MCHI) (FXI) exports of solar products, lithium-ion batteries and electric vehicles surged to a record $21.9B in March amid the Iran war, signaling a sharp acceleration in shipments of the country’s key clean energy technologies.

The combined total was up 70% from March 2025, according to Nicolas Fulghum of energy-focused think tank Ember. The jump coincided with high oil (BNO) (USO) and gas (UNG) prices driven by tensions in the Middle East, which appeared to intensify global interest in alternatives tied to electrification.

Changes to Chinese export rebates for solar (TAN) (RAYS) and batteries (LIT) (BATT) also contributed to the March surge. Battery and electric vehicle (DRIV) (KARS) exports both pushed higher, while solar shipments jumped as well, lifting the group total to an all-time high.

The data suggest the latest fossil fuel shock may already be feeding into global demand for clean energy equipment through near-term trade flows, in addition to long-term policy channels.

data.title: Exelon cut at Jefferies after Pennsylvania rate case 'own goal'
data.description:
Exelon (EXC) down 0.8% in Monday's trading as Jefferies downgraded the utility to Hold from Buy with a $50 price target, trimmed from $55, following the negative Pennsylvania PECO rate case withdrawal, which the bank said indicates a more challenging affordability backdrop.

Jefferies analyst Paul Zimbardo said he views Exelon (EXC) as generally attractively valued at a ~15% estimated 2028 P/E discount to peers, but the majority of the company's rate base is expected to have rate case reviews in 2027, primarily in Pennsylvania and Illinois.

After the PECO rate case withdrawal and December 2023 Illinois shock negative rate case, investors are unlikely to give Exelon (EXC) the benefit of the doubt, locking in a large discount, Zimbardo said.

The passage of utility affordability legislation in Maryland also highlights the contentious affordability environment in Exelon's (EXC) service territories, Zimbardo wrote, adding he does not see a positive catalyst path to cause the shares to re-rate higher, even at the ~15% P/E discount; on the contrary, the analyst sees more cautious headline risk than favorable developments in 2026.

data.title: JetBlue's CEO dismisses bankruptcy speculation
data.description:
JetBlue Airways (JBLU) CEO Joanna Geraghty reassured employees in an internal memo that the carrier is not considering filing for bankruptcy protection this year. The comments were in reaction to speculation that the high cost of jet fuel would pressure the company's liquidity.

Geraghty noted that JetBlue (JBLU) has sufficient access to additional capital, including a recent $500M loan backed by aircraft, with an option to raise another $250M.

In 2025, JetBlue (JVLU) posted a net loss of about $602M with operating revenue around $9.1B and liquidity of $2.5B, indicating pressure but not an immediate liquidity crisis.

The speculation about JetBlue's (JBLU) financial position ramped up after founder David Neeleman publicly warned that if jet fuel prices average about $4.50 per gallon, JetBlue (JBLU) could lose around $1.3B in 2026.

Shares of JetBlue (JBLU) are up 28% on a year-to-date basis, indicating confidence from many investors. However, short interest on JetBlue (JBLU) stands at 18% of the total float.

data.title: MetLife Q1 2026 dividend preview: 13-year streak set for another raise
data.description:
MetLife (MET) is expected to announce a dividend increase this April, continuing its 13-year streak of regular dividend growth.

Based on past trends, analysts expect a consensus annual dividend of about ~$2.35 per share. This equals a quarterly dividend of ~0.5875 per share, which implies an increase of ~3.5% from the current ~$0.5675 per share payout.

The company last paid a dividend of ~$0.5675 per share in January 2026, which comes to an annual yield of 2.91%.

Earlier, in April last year, it increased the dividend by ~4.1%, from ~$0.5450 to ~$0.5675.

Over the past five years, MetLife (MET) has delivered a dividend growth rate of ~4.29% and currently maintains a payout ratio of ~25.42%.

Meanwhile, on dividend quality metrics, the company carries ratings of B- for safety, C- for growth, B+ for yield, and A- for consistency.

MetLife (MET) is set to release its Q1 2026 financial results on May 6, 2026, after the market closes, with a conference call on May 7, 2026.

data.title: MP Materials launched Outperform at Wedbush as 'America's rare earth national champion'
data.description:
MP Materials (MP) up 5.5% in Monday's trading as Wedbush initiated coverage with an Outperform rating and $90 price target, noting the company's Mountain Pass asset in California is one of only four mines supplying 80% of the world's rare earths and the only such mine on U.S. soil, making it "nearly irreplaceable."

As rare earth supply chain independence has moved to the top of the U.S. national security agenda amid escalating tensions with China, — which controls ~60% of global mining and 90% of downstream processing and magnet manufacturing — MP Materials (MP) has emerged as "America's designated rare earth national champion," Wedbush analyst Dan Ives and Sam Brandeis said.

Vertical integration is "the most underappreciated dimension of the MP story," and the cross-pollination between Mountain Pass and Independence creates cost and quality advantages that flow in both directions, the analysts said.

With Apple, General Motors, and the U.S. Department of Defense anchoring the commercial pipeline and the 10X facility in Northlake, Texas, on track for 2028 commissioning, Ives and Brandeis said MP's (MP) opportunity is "uniquely derisked, with contracted demand, a sovereign customer, and dated production catalysts all converging over the next few years."

Wedbush also initiated USA Rare Earth (USAR) with an Outperform rating and $29 price target; shares are up 10.8% after announcing an acquisition of Brazil's Serra Verde Group in a $2.8B cash and stock transaction, in one of the largest-ever deals in the rare earths industry.

data.title: Software sector is ‘structurally in trouble’ – Trivariate Research’s Adam Parker
data.description:
Adam Parker, founder and CEO of Trivariate Research, is warning investors that the software sector (IGV), (XSW), (IGPT) is “structurally in trouble” even as the broader tech market continues its upward climb.

In an interview with CNBC, Parker made clear his investment preference: “I’m still semis over software. That’s our North Star.”

Despite his bearish stance on software, Parker remains optimistic about tech earnings overall. Bottom-up estimates for the tech sector project 43% year-over-year growth in 2026 and another 25% in 2027, driven largely by memory chips and NVIDIA (NVDA).

Even if those estimates prove too aggressive, Parker argues the absolute level of earnings is high enough to support current valuations.

Parker pointed to historical research from Trivariate that examined 100 years of market data. Since 1928, there have been 22 instances of consecutive double-digit earnings growth, and only five of those first years resulted in negative market returns. Those exceptions included major events like the Great Depression and World War II, suggesting that strong earnings typically help markets climb the “wall of worry.”

The Trivariate CEO explained his preference for semiconductors (SMH), (SOXX) over software by citing the higher “obsolescence risk” facing software companies today.

However, he pushed back against concerns that artificial intelligence investments have become overheated.

“I don’t think AI is in a bubble. I think if people think that, they don’t really know what it is,” Parker said.

One factor that has tempered Parker’s optimism is the asymmetric response to earnings results. Companies that miss expectations are seeing their stocks fall far more than companies that beat are being rewarded. Parker also acknowledged the possibility of a data center overbuild in four or five years, which could create headwinds.

Ultimately, Parker believes the earnings power of the semiconductor complex is too robust to justify a negative outlook on the broader market.

While he expects software multiples to contract and some companies to struggle, he sees the foundational strength of tech earnings as a significant buffer.

“There’s just too much earnings power to get too negative,” he concluded.

Semiconductor ETFs: (SMH), (SOXX), (SOXL), (FTXL), (XSD), (USD), (PSI), and (SEMI).

Artificial Intelligence/Robotics ETFs: (AIQ), (BOTZ), (DTEC), (WTAI), (XAIX), (WISE), (GINN), (ROBT), (TECB), (XT), (THNQ), and (CHAT).

Tech ETFs: (VGT), (XLK), (IYW), (FTEC), (IXN), and (RSPT).

data.title: Southern Co raises dividend by 2.7% to $0.76
data.description:
• Southern Co (SO) declares $0.76/share quarterly dividend, 2.7% increase from prior dividend of $0.74.
  • Forward yield 3.23%
  • Payable June 8; for shareholders of record May 18; ex-div May 18.
  • See SO Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Trump administration opens $166B tariff refund process after court loss
data.description:
The Trump administration has begun accepting claims from importers seeking refunds on more than $166 billion in tariffs invalidated by the Supreme Court, launching what could become one of the largest trade-related repayments in U.S. history, the New York Times reported Monday.

Businesses that paid the duties can now apply through a new federal portal to recover the tariffs, along with interest. The levies had been imposed last year under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act, a statute the Court ruled did not authorize the president to impose broad import tariffs.

For investors, the refund process matters because it could inject liquidity back into companies that absorbed tariff costs, especially retailers, manufacturers, logistics firms and small importers. It also removes a sizable revenue source from the federal government while leaving uncertainty over whether replacement tariffs may soon be introduced through other legal channels.

More than 3,000 businesses, including FedEx (FDX) and Costco (COST), had already sued to secure refunds before the claims system opened. Customs officials previously said payments could take 60 to 90 days after claims are accepted, though trade lawyers warned delays or technical issues are possible.

Some companies have indicated they may pass savings to customers through lower prices or direct reimbursements, but economists said many firms are likely to retain cash while awaiting possible new tariffs.

The White House has not ruled out further legal or policy steps as it explores alternate tariff authorities under existing trade laws.
